title: The Inception Module
concept: An Inception module splits one input feature map into parallel 1x1, 3x3, 5x5, and pooling branches, then concatenates all branch outputs together along the channel dimension.

exercises.prompt: Recap: In GoogLeNet's basic idea, what do the parallel branches mostly differ in?

options.text: The scale of patterns they can emphasize
The language spoken by the image
Whether the image still has pixels

exercises.explain: Correct — each branch is a different scale-sensitive path through the same input.

cards.title: One road splits into four lanes
cards.body: An Inception module is easier to understand if you picture a road split. One incoming feature map enters the block, the traffic separates into several lanes that do different work, and then everything merges back into one outgoing feature stack.

cards.title: What is inside the module?
cards.body: A classic Inception module has several side-by-side branches, commonly a 1x1 branch, a 3x3 branch, a 5x5 branch, and a pooling branch. All branches process the same input feature map. Their outputs are then concatenated, meaning stacked together along channel depth.

cards.title: Several checkout lanes, one shared store
cards.body: Imagine one line at a store splitting into four checkout lanes. Each lane handles customers in a slightly different way, but nobody throws away a lane's result. When the lanes finish, all processed customers rejoin the same store flow. Concatenation is that rejoining step for channel stacks.

cards.title: How concatenation changes depth
cards.body: Suppose all four branches keep the same height and width, but output different channel counts: 1x1 -> 16 channels, 3x3 -> 24, 5x5 -> 8, and pool -> 8. After concatenation, the output is still the same spatial size, but now it has 16 + 24 + 8 + 8 = 56 channels.

cards.title: Concatenate does not mean average
cards.body: The branch outputs are not averaged into one blurry result, and the network does not pick a single winning branch. Concatenation keeps all branch outputs by stacking them deeper, so later layers can use whichever branch responses help most.

cards.title: Split, process, stack
cards.body: The key shape idea is simple: same height and width, deeper channel stack after the merge.

right.text:
All branch channels kept
Stacked along depth
One richer feature map out

exercises.prompt: After the 1x1, 3x3, 5x5, and pooling branches finish, what usually happens next in an Inception module?

options.text: Their outputs are concatenated into one deeper feature map
The largest branch deletes the others
The image is converted into text tokens
The spatial height and width of each branch are added together

exercises.explain: Right — the branch outputs are stacked in channel depth to form one outgoing feature map.

exercises.prompt: True or false: concatenation in an Inception module usually means stacking branch outputs along the channel dimension, not placing them side-by-side across the image.
exercises.explain: True — the spatial grid stays aligned while depth grows.

exercises.sentence: At the end of an Inception module, the branch outputs are ___ together along channel depth.
exercises.explain: Exactly — concatenation is the merge step that keeps all branch outputs.

exercises.prompt: Which statement correctly describes the pooling branch inside an Inception module?

options.text: It is one more parallel path that contributes its own output channels before the final concatenation
It cancels the other branches and replaces them completely
It exists only outside the module and never as a branch

exercises.explain: Yes — pooling is part of the side-by-side module story, not a separate afterthought.

exercises.prompt: An Inception module's four branches output 8, 16, 16, and 8 channels, all with the same height and width. After concatenation, how many channels does the module output?
exercises.explain: Correct — concatenation keeps every branch output, so the depths add: 8 + 16 + 16 + 8 = 48. Next: why 1x1 convolutions are the quiet heroes inside those branches.
